question: Why is the baryon asymmetry of the universe a fundamental problem?

options: Observed $\eta_B = (n_B - n_{\bar B})/n_\gamma \approx 6\times 10^{-10}$; standard cosmology starts $B = 0$ at early times; SM with CKM CP only gives $\eta \sim 10^{-20}$, factor $10^{10}$ short. Requires Sakharov conditions: B violation + C/CP violation + departure from equilibrium. BSM physics needed
Universe is matter-antimatter symmetric; no asymmetry (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Asymmetry trivially generated by inflation (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect)
Sakharov conditions automatically satisfied in SM (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Observation: universe contains baryons, virtually no antibaryons (anti-stars constrained $< 10^{-7}$ in our galaxy via gamma rays). Asymmetry parametrised by $\eta_B$. Big Bang model: at $T \gg $ GeV, in thermal equilibrium baryons and antibaryons equal. After annihilation, residual baryons survive — if $\eta_B = 0$ initially, no matter today. SM contributions: (1) sphalerons violate $B + L$ but conserve $B - L$; (2) CKM CP gives small CP violation; (3) EW phase transition is crossover (with $m_h = 125$ GeV), not first-order, fails 'out-of-equilibrium' Sakharov condition. Net SM prediction: $\eta \sim 10^{-20}$ — $10^{10}\times$ too small. BSM mechanisms required: leptogenesis (most popular), EW baryogenesis with BSM CP/transition, Affleck-Dine, GUT baryogenesis.

active.prompt: Match each baryogenesis scenario with its mechanism:

pairs.definition: Heavy RH neutrino decays CP-asymmetrically → lepton asymmetry → sphalerons partially convert to baryons
1st-order EW phase transition at $T \sim 100$ GeV; bubble walls + CP-violation generate B asymmetry; requires BSM
SUSY flat direction acquires VEV during inflation; coherent oscillation produces baryons via Q-balls etc.
Heavy X-boson decays violating B at GUT scale; problem: inflation washes away if inflation scale below GUT

active.explanation: Each scenario has trade-offs. Leptogenesis: clean prediction connected to neutrino masses; testable in principle via $\nu$ properties. EW baryogenesis: testable now at LHC + EDMs (needs new CP-violation at TeV); minimal SM ruled out by Higgs mass. Affleck-Dine: SUSY-specific. GUT: hard to test directly. No experimental confirmation of any.

question: Sphalerons in the SM: what are they and what do they do?

options: Non-perturbative EW field configurations interpolating between topologically distinct vacua; violate $B + L$ by $2N_f = 6$ units per transition. Rate exponentially suppressed at $T = 0$ ($\sim e^{-16\pi^2/g^2}$) but thermally active at $T > $ EW scale; equilibrate $B$ and $L$ asymmetries in early universe — basis for converting lepton to baryon asymmetry in leptogenesis
Sphalerons are gauge-boson resonances at LHC (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

Sphalerons conserve all quantum numbers (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)

$B$-violation forbidden in SM (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Sphaleron (Klinkhamer-Manton 1984): unstable static saddle-point solution of EW field equations, energy $E_{\rm sph} \sim 4\pi v/g \approx 9$ TeV. Transitions between topological vacua change Chern-Simons number; via anomaly $\partial_\mu j_{B+L}^\mu = (g^2 N_f/16\pi^2)W\tilde W$ → $\Delta(B+L) = 2N_f \Delta N_{CS} = 6$ per transition. $B - L$ conserved. At $T = 0$: instanton-suppressed, $\Gamma \sim e^{-16\pi^2/g^2} \sim 10^{-170}$ — irrelevant. At $T > E_{\rm sph}/T \sim T_{EW}$: thermal sphalerons active, $\Gamma/V \sim \alpha_W^5 T^4$ — rapid in early universe, equilibrate $B+L \to 0$ on cosmological timescale. After EW transition: turn off quickly. Crucial for leptogenesis: $L$ → mix of $B, L$ via sphalerons (fraction depends on SM degrees of freedom).

options: Heavy Majorana RH neutrinos $N_i$ (masses $\sim 10^9-10^{14}$ GeV, see-saw partners of light $\nu$) decay CP-asymmetrically: $\Gamma(N \to LH) \neq \Gamma(N \to \bar L H^*)$ via interference of tree + loop diagrams; produces lepton asymmetry $Y_L$. Sphalerons partially convert $Y_L \to Y_B$ (factor $\sim 28/79$ in SM). Yields $\eta_B \sim 10^{-10}$ for $M_1 \gtrsim 10^9$ GeV (Davidson-Ibarra bound)

Lepton asymmetry generated by Higgs decays alone (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Leptogenesis requires light neutrinos to be Dirac (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Mechanism produces baryons directly, no leptons (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence)
passive.explanation: Fukugita-Yanagida (1986): see-saw mechanism implies heavy RH neutrinos; their decays in early universe can satisfy Sakharov. (1) $N_i$ Majorana → $L$ violated by 2; (2) loop interference in $N \to LH$ with internal $N_j, N_k$ gives CP-violating decay asymmetry $\varepsilon_i = (\Gamma_i - \bar\Gamma_i)/(\Gamma_i + \bar\Gamma_i)$; (3) decay out of equilibrium when $\Gamma_N < H$ at $T \sim M_N$. Sakharov satisfied. Asymmetry generated at $T \sim M_1 \gtrsim 10^9$ GeV; sphalerons (active until $T \sim 130$ GeV) convert $L$ to $B$ partially: $Y_B = (28/79)Y_{B-L}$. Davidson-Ibarra: $|\varepsilon_1| \le (3 M_1 \Delta m_{\rm atm}^2)/(8\pi v^2)$ → require $M_1 \gtrsim 10^9$ GeV for sufficient asymmetry. Resonant leptogenesis: degenerate $N_i$ enhance $\varepsilon$, allows lower scale (TeV).

question: Electroweak baryogenesis: why is it ruled out in minimal SM and what does revival require?

options: Needs 1st-order EW phase transition (bubble-wall mechanism); SM with $m_h = 125$ GeV gives crossover not 1st-order. Also CKM CP violation gives $\eta \sim 10^{-20}$. Revival requires BSM: 2HDM (two Higgs doublets) for stronger transition + new CP phases; singlet scalars; SUSY (stop-driven, ruled out); composite Higgs. Probes: collider Higgs precision, EDMs

EW baryogenesis works perfectly in SM (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general)

SM EW transition is naturally 1st-order with current Higgs mass (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

EW baryogenesis ruled out for all extensions (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Mechanism: 1st-order EW PT at $T \sim 100$ GeV creates bubble nucleation with broken phase inside. CP-violating interactions in bubble walls separate $B$ from $\bar B$; sphalerons in unbroken phase outside equilibrate. Asymmetry frozen inside expanding bubbles. Requirements: (1) sufficiently strong 1st-order PT ($v_c/T_c \gtrsim 1$ for sphaleron suppression inside); (2) new CP phases ($\delta_{\rm new} \sim O(1)$, vs.\ CKM $\sim 10^{-15}$ effective). SM with $m_h = 125$ GeV: lattice + perturbative calculations give crossover, no 1st-order PT — fails (1). CKM gives $\eta \sim 10^{-20}$ — fails (2). BSM extensions: 2HDM (Lee 1973), MSSM with light stop (now ruled out by LHC stop $m \gtrsim 1$ TeV), SM + singlet scalar, composite Higgs. Constraints: precision Higgs couplings (LHC), EDMs (ACME), tightening allowed parameter space. Future colliders (FCC-ee, ILC) Higgs trilinear coupling — discriminator.

question: Affleck-Dine mechanism: how does it generate baryons via SUSY flat directions?

options: MSSM has 'flat directions' in field space where scalar potential vanishes classically; combinations of squarks/sleptons (e.g.\ $udd$ or $LH_u$) can carry $B$ or $L$. During inflation, these acquire large VEVs; after inflation, complex VEV rotates in field space → asymmetry in net $B$/$L$. Can give large asymmetry naturally; often forms Q-balls

Affleck-Dine requires no SUSY (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Mechanism produces antimatter only (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Generates asymmetry via Higgs sector alone (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Affleck-Dine (1985): MSSM has D-flat directions where $D_a = \sum_i q_i\phi_i\phi_i^* = 0$ (gauge-invariant moduli); explicit examples $LH_u$, $udd$, $\bar u\bar d\bar d$ etc. Carry $B, L$ quantum numbers. Inflation: SUSY-breaking corrections induce mass $\sim H_{\rm inf}$ for moduli; if negative, flat direction acquires large VEV $\phi \sim M_{\rm Pl}$. After inflation, when $H \sim m_{3/2}$: usual SUSY soft masses kick in (positive mass squared, A-term + Hermitian conjugate breaks $U(1)_{B-L}$); field starts rotating in complex plane. Rotation = baryon current $J^\mu = -i(\phi^*\partial^\mu\phi - \partial^\mu\phi^*\phi)$. Decay of $\phi$ into quarks deposits asymmetry. Often forms Q-balls (non-topological solitons carrying $B$); may be DM. Affleck-Dine baryogenesis: very efficient, easily over-produces — needs tuning.

question: Connection between leptogenesis and neutrino observables: what can future experiments probe?

options: Leptogenesis CP violation in heavy $N$ decays connected (in restricted scenarios) to PMNS phases of light neutrinos. Generic connection broken by 'orthogonal matrix' freedom; thermal leptogenesis predicts $M_1 \gtrsim 10^9$ GeV (Davidson-Ibarra bound) — out of LHC reach. Testable via: (a) leptonic CP phase $\delta$ (DUNE, Hyper-K); (b) $0\nu\beta\beta$ Majorana mass (KamLAND-Zen, LEGEND); (c) low-scale resonant leptogenesis at colliders

No connection between leptogenesis and observables (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

Leptogenesis fully predicted by current PMNS measurement (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)

$N_1$ mass measurable at next LHC run (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation:
See-saw parametrisation (Casas-Ibarra): $m_D = \sqrt{M_R} R\sqrt{m_
u} U_{m PMNS}^\dagger$ with $R$ complex orthogonal matrix containing 3 extra complex parameters not in light-$
u$ data. So leptogenesis CP asymmetries depend on phases in $R$ uncorrelated with PMNS $\delta$. Special scenarios (e.g.\ minimal seesaw $N_f = 2$ + flavour structure): correlations emerge. Predictions: (i) successful leptogenesis requires complex $R$, but doesn't pin $\delta_{m PMNS}$; (ii) Majorana nature of light $
u$ implied if leptogenesis from $N$ ($L$ violated by 2) — testable via $0
u eta eta$; (iii) sum $\sum m_
u \lesssim 0.1$ eV consistent. Resonant leptogenesis at TeV: heavy $N$ at LHC, displaced vertices; CP asymmetry in same-sign dilepton signals. Future: combine $\delta_{CP}$ (DUNE), $0
u eta eta$ (LEGEND-1000), LHC searches — narrow viable region.
